title: Pet Urine on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Pet urine is more concentrated than human urine — higher protein, uric acid, and pheromone content. On upholstery, uric acid crystallizes as it dries and bonds tightly to fibers. These crystals reactivate with humidity, causing recurring odor. Cat urine is particularly concentrated with felinine (a sulfur-containing amino acid) that produces especially persistent odor through bacterial decomposition.
whyThisWorks: Enzyme treatment is essential — uric acid crystals resist standard cleaning. The enzyme breaks down the protein and uric acid bonds on upholstery. Cold water prevents protein from setting. For odor, the enzyme must penetrate to the base of the contamination, not just the surface. Complete saturation of the affected area is necessary for odor resolution.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing/foam damage

homeSolutions.instruction: Find the tag sewn into a seam under a cushion. One letter: W = water-safe, S = solvent only, WS = both okay, X = vacuum only. On S or X, stop and call a pro — water will ring the fabric. On W or WS, proceed. No tag? Treat as S and call a pro. Enzyme cleaners (which you'll need in step 6) require water compatibility.

homeSolutions.agent: Find the full spot — blacklight it if you can
homeSolutions.instruction: Pet urine on a cushion always spreads bigger than you can see, and if the cushion cover comes off, the pad under it is usually also contaminated. In a dark room, sweep a UV blacklight across the area — urine glows pale yellow-green. Mark edges with painter's tape. Also check the back of the cushion and the frame below; accidents often leak through. Treating only the visible spot is why odor comes back.

Two rules: (1) no hot water — heat sets the protein and bakes the odor in forever. (2) No ammonia cleaners — they smell like urine to your pet and trigger re-marking right where you just cleaned. Skip the vinegar + baking soda pairing — they cancel each other out. The real fix is an enzyme cleaner that digests the uric acid crystals.

homeSolutions.agent: Press fresh urine out with paper towels
homeSolutions.instruction: If it's fresh: stack 6–8 sheets of white paper towels over the area, press down with your full weight (stand on them if the cushion can handle it, wearing a shoe you don't love). Replace and repeat until the towels come up nearly dry. If it's already dried: lay a cold DAMP (not dripping) white towel over it for 10 minutes to rehydrate before anything else.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with two cups of cold water in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, press on the area. Let sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn lifts surface residue and any trace fats so the enzyme in step 6 can reach the uric acid. Skip OxiClean and baking soda — alkaline products set protein and neutralize enzymes.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme cleaner — saturate enough to reach the cushion pad
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply a pet-specific enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie, Simple Solution, Anti Icky Poo). On upholstery this is tricky: you DO need to saturate enough to reach the pad, but NOT so much that you create water rings or soak the cushion through. Start with enough to make the surface visibly wet but not puddled. If you can unzip the cushion cover, treat both the cover AND the foam pad underneath separately.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Enzymes are deactivated by heat, chlorine bleach, and strong alkaline cleaners. Don't mix. No steam cleaning during the enzyme stage.

homeSolutions.agent: Covered dwell — 8 to 24 hours, stay damp
homeSolutions.instruction: Lay a damp (not dripping) white towel over the treated area and cover with plastic wrap. Tape the edges to seal. This keeps the enzyme working at full moisture instead of drying on the surface. 8 hours for a fresh accident; 24 for older spots. The enzyme is digesting uric acid — rushing this step is why home treatments fail.

Uncover, blot surface moisture. Point a room fan at the area for 12–24 hours (longer on upholstery than carpet — foam dries slowly). Once dry, nose-check up close and re-sweep the blacklight. If odor or fluorescence remains, repeat steps 6–7 with fresh enzyme. Don't let the pet or anyone sit on the cushion until it's fully dry and odor-free — damp + warm body = perfect re-marking trigger.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — especially for leaked-through or cushion-pad contamination
homeSolutions.instruction: If odor returns with humidity, the blacklight still lights up, or the accident soaked through to the cushion foam, call a pro upholstery cleaner. Tell them: "Pet urine on [W/WS/unknown] upholstery, treated with Dawn + enzyme (2 rounds), no heat, no ammonia used. Odor or blacklight signal still present — possible cushion-pad contamination." Pros have low-moisture extraction + sub-surface enzyme injection; in worst cases the cushion foam gets replaced (much cheaper than replacing the couch).

steps: 1. Blot up as much liquid as possible with clean white cloths — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 2 cups cold water. Apply to stain and blot
3. Mix equal parts white vinegar and cold water. Apply to neutralize odor
4. Sprinkle baking soda over the area to absorb remaining moisture and odor — let sit 15-30 min
5. Blot thoroughly and air dry — no heat
products: Enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle or Rocco & Roxie)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Do NOT use OxiClean or oxidizing agents on protein stains — they can set the stain permanently

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is pet urine on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
